issues.fixed: If you installed Claude Code via npm globally ([CODE]) and also via the install script, you'll have two competing binaries. Check which one is active:

issues.original: This is the part most users never read. Claude Code merges configuration from three locations, in increasing precedence:
issues.fixed: This is the part most users never read. Claude Code merges configuration from three locations, in order of increasing precedence:

issues.original: Why three layers and not two? Because some settings are personal-and-global (your default model, your editor command), some are team-and-shared (the linter the project uses, the test command), and some are personal-but-project-scoped (your own API key for the staging server).
issues.fixed: Why three layers and not two? Some settings are personal and global (like your default model and editor command), some are team and shared (like the linter and test command), and some are personal but project-scoped (like your API key for the staging server).

issues.original: The merge is a deep merge with later layers winning. If your machine config sets [CODE] and the project config doesn't mention [CODE] at all, you get Opus. If the project config sets [CODE], the project wins — unless your local config overrides it again.
issues.fixed: The merge is a deep merge, with later layers taking precedence. If your machine config sets [CODE] and the project config doesn't mention [CODE] at all, you get Opus. If the project config sets [CODE], the project wins — unless your local config overrides it again.

issues.original: For the [CODE] object, arrays are concatenated. If the project allows [CODE] and your machine config allows [CODE], you get both. Deny rules always take precedence over allow rules regardless of layer.
issues.fixed: For the [CODE] object, arrays are concatenated. If the project allows [CODE] and your machine config allows [CODE], you get both. Deny rules always take precedence over allow rules, regardless of layer.

issues.original: Those are read-only commands I never want to be prompted for. Every time Claude asks "can I run [CODE]?" it interrupts my flow for zero safety benefit. Put read-only operations here and forget about them.
issues.fixed: These are read-only commands I never want to be prompted for. Every time Claude asks "can I run [CODE]?" it interrupts my flow without any safety benefit. Put read-only operations here and forget about them.

issues.original: What I don't put here: anything that writes files, runs tests, or executes project-specific commands. Those belong in the project layer.
issues.fixed: I don't put anything that writes files, runs tests, or executes project-specific commands here. Those belong in the project layer.

issues.original: A real-world example from a Next.js project:
issues.fixed: A real-world example from a Next.js project:

issues.original: The deny list is as important as the allow list. [CODE] in a Claude session would be catastrophic. [CODE] against production is not something you want an AI to do autonomously. Be explicit about what's off-limits.
issues.fixed: The deny list is as important as the allow list. [CODE] in a Claude session would be catastrophic. [CODE] against production is not something you want an AI to do autonomously. Be explicit about what's off-limits.

issues.original: Hooks let you run shell commands before or after Claude uses specific tools. The [CODE] hook fires before a tool runs, [CODE] after. You can use them for logging, notifications, or validation.
issues.fixed: Hooks let you run shell commands before or after Claude uses specific tools. The [CODE] hook runs before a tool, and [CODE] runs after. Use them for logging, notifications, or validation.

issues.original: Environment variables set here are available to Claude's shell commands in that project. This is the right place for API keys, database URLs, and anything else that's both sensitive and project-specific.
issues.fixed: Environment variables set here are available to Claude's shell commands in that project. This is the right place for API keys, database URLs, and other sensitive, project-specific data.

issues.original: A common mistake: putting sensitive values in the project-level config and committing them. The three-layer system exists specifically so you don't have to do that.
issues.fixed: A common mistake is putting sensitive values in the project-level config and committing them. The three-layer system exists to prevent this.

issues.original: Type [CODE] at the start of a line and the rest of the line is appended to the project's memory file ([CODE]) instead of being sent as a prompt. Example:
issues.fixed: Type [CODE] at the start of a line, and the rest of the line is appended to the project's memory file ([CODE]) instead of being sent as a prompt. Example:

issues.original: Claude doesn't reply. It opens an editor on [CODE], drops the line in, and saves. The next conversation starts with that line in context.
issues.fixed: Claude doesn't reply. It opens an editor on [CODE], adds the line, and saves. The next conversation starts with that line in context.

issues.original: The whole point of the system is that you write your own, and they live in your repo.
issues.fixed: The main point of the system is for you to write your own commands, which live in your repo.

issues.original: After creation you have to restart Claude Code (one of the few places it's not hot-reloaded).
issues.fixed: After creating the command, you need to restart Claude Code (one of the few places it's not hot-reloaded).

issues.original: You get a structured audit report instead of having to remember the three commands and their order.
issues.fixed: You get a structured audit report without having to remember the three commands and their order.

issues.original: There's no DSL, no special syntax. That keeps the surface area tiny.
issues.fixed: There's no DSL or special syntax, which keeps the surface area small.

issues.original: This means a team can override your personal [CODE] with a project-specific version.
issues.fixed: This means a team can override your personal [CODE] with a project-specific one.

issues.original: It can reference files with [CODE], include thinking level triggers ('think a lot'), use [CODE] for parameterization, contain multi-step instructions, and preserve Markdown formatting.
issues.fixed: It can reference files with [CODE], include thinking level triggers ('think a lot'), use [CODE] for parameterization, contain multi-step instructions, and preserve Markdown formatting.

issues.original: Personal commands are available in every project. Team commands are specific to one repo.
issues.fixed: Personal commands are available in every project, while team commands are specific to one repo.

issues.original: If someone can't guess that [CODE] reviews code, the naming is wrong.
issues.fixed: If someone can't guess that [CODE] reviews code, the naming is incorrect.

issues.original: Git history preserves the old version if you ever need it back.
issues.fixed: Git history preserves the old version if you need it later.

issues.original: Everything after [CODE] becomes [CODE], so Claude gets 'axios vs fetch vs ky for HTTP requests' as a natural language string.
issues.fixed: Everything after [CODE] becomes [CODE], so Claude gets 'axios vs fetch vs ky for HTTP requests' as a natural language string.

issues.original: Design your prompts to handle this gracefully: This pattern — 'if arguments exist, scope to them; otherwise, do everything' — makes commands more flexible without needing separate commands for the general and specific cases.
issues.fixed: Design your prompts to handle this gracefully. This pattern — 'if arguments exist, scope to them; otherwise, do everything' — makes commands more flexible without needing separate commands for general and specific cases.

issues.original: Step 4 is not a joke. The next time anyone runs [CODE] in that repo and types [CODE], they see the command in the autocomplete list. They try it. It works. They use it again. No training deck, no Confluence page, no onboarding session.
issues.fixed: Step 4 is not a joke. The next time anyone runs [CODE] in that repo and types [CODE], they see the command in the autocomplete list. They try it, it works, and they use it again. No training deck, no Confluence page, no onboarding session.

issues.original: At this point, the commands are effectively the team's shared vocabulary for interacting with AI.
issues.fixed: At this point, the commands are the team's shared vocabulary for interacting with AI.

issues.original: Each command file is a runnable specification of a workflow. That's more useful than a wiki page because it's always up to date (if it weren't, people would fix it because they use it every day).
issues.fixed: Each command file is a runnable specification of a workflow, which is more useful than a wiki page because it's always up to date (if it weren't, people would fix it because they use it every day).

issues.original: If MCP is how Claude reaches out, Hooks are how you reach in. They are the way to enforce — not just hope for — the rules you care about.
issues.fixed: If MCP is how Claude reaches out, hooks are how you reach in. They enforce the rules you care about, not just hope for them.

issues.original: A Hook is a shell command. Claude Code runs it at one of a few well-defined moments. The two you'll use most:
issues.fixed: A hook is a shell command that Claude Code runs at specific moments. The two you'll use most:

issues.original: There are others ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]). For day-to-day work, those two are 90% of the value.
issues.fixed: There are others, but these two cover 90% of your day-to-day needs.

issues.original: Let me walk through exactly what happens when Claude calls a tool and hooks are configured. Understanding this lifecycle is key to writing hooks that work correctly.
issues.fixed: Let's walk through what happens when Claude calls a tool and hooks are configured. Understanding this lifecycle is key to writing effective hooks.

issues.original: When Claude decides to call a tool, this sequence runs:
issues.fixed: When Claude decides to call a tool, this sequence runs:

issues.original: Claude Code resolves the tool name and input arguments. It checks the [CODE] hooks in settings. Each hook with a matching [CODE] is selected. Selected hooks run **sequentially**, in the order they appear in the config. Each hook receives the tool call details as JSON on **stdin**. If **any** hook exits with a non-zero code, the tool call is **blocked**. The hook's stderr output is sent back to the model as an error message. If all hooks exit with code 0, the tool call proceeds.
issues.fixed: Claude Code resolves the tool name and input arguments, then checks the [CODE] hooks in settings. Each hook with a matching [CODE] is selected. Selected hooks run sequentially, in the order they appear in the config. Each hook receives the tool call details as JSON on stdin. If any hook exits with a non-zero code, the tool call is blocked, and the hook's stderr output is sent back to the model as an error message. If all hooks exit with code 0, the tool call proceeds.

issues.original: Runs before the user's prompt is sent to the model. Use it to: Warn about prompts that contain sensitive data Add standard context to every prompt Log prompts for audit
issues.fixed: Runs before the user's prompt is sent to the model. Use it to: warn about prompts with sensitive data, add standard context to every prompt, and log prompts for audit.

issues.original: Runs when Claude finishes its response and is about to yield control back to the user. Useful for: Generating summary reports Cleaning up temporary files Sending notifications ("Claude finished the task")
issues.fixed: Runs when Claude finishes its response and is about to yield control back to the user. Use it to: generate summary reports, clean up temporary files, and send notifications (e.g., "Claude finished the task").

issues.original: Runs when Claude Code sends a desktop notification (e.g., when a long task completes). You can use this to route notifications to other channels — Slack, email, webhook.
issues.fixed: Runs when Claude Code sends a desktop notification (e.g., when a long task completes). Use it to route notifications to other channels like Slack, email, or webhooks.

issues.original: Hooks are shell commands, not scripts. The [CODE] field is passed to the system shell ([CODE]). This means you can use shell features like pipes and redirects directly: But for anything beyond a one-liner, use a script file.
issues.fixed: Hooks are shell commands, not scripts. The [CODE] field is passed to the system shell ([CODE]), so you can use shell features like pipes and redirects directly. For anything more complex, use a script file.

issues.original: Each hook must finish before the next one starts. Five hooks at 200ms each is a full second of delay on every tool call. Keep hooks fast.
issues.fixed: Each hook must finish before the next one starts. Five hooks at 200ms each add up to a full second of delay per tool call. Keep hooks fast.

issues.original: Chapter 5 was the conceptual tour of hooks. This one is the field guide. Out of the 100-script reference repo, ten earn their place in every serious project I run. Those are the ten I will walk through, with code.
issues.fixed: Chapter 5 provided a conceptual tour of hooks. This chapter is the field guide. From the 100-script reference repo, ten scripts earn their place in every serious project I run. I'll walk through these ten with code.

issues.original: Before we dig in, let me lay out the hook lifecycle so the code that follows makes sense:
issues.fixed: Before we dive in, here's the hook lifecycle to make the following code clear:

issues.original: The common preamble for all hooks: I will skip that preamble in some listings below for brevity, but every real hook starts with it.
issues.fixed: The common preamble for all hooks: I'll skip this preamble in some listings for brevity, but every real hook starts with it.

issues.original: Every hook is a script that reads a JSON payload from stdin and signals back via exit code (verdict) and stderr (explanation). The matcher in settings.json picks which hooks see each tool call.
issues.fixed: Each hook is a script that reads a JSON payload from stdin and signals back via exit code (verdict) and stderr (explanation). The matcher in settings.json determines which hooks handle each tool call.

issues.original: The single highest-ROI hook. Stops [CODE] and [CODE] from touching [CODE], [CODE], [CODE].
issues.fixed: The highest-ROI hook. It stops [CODE] and [CODE] from accessing [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE].

issues.original: The most common foot-gun. PreToolUse on [CODE].
issues.fixed: The most common foot-gun. Use PreToolUse on [CODE].

issues.original: The inverse of the blacklist, for repos that touch production. Allow only an explicit set of binaries.
issues.fixed: The inverse of the blacklist, for repos that touch production. It allows only an explicit set of binaries.

issues.original: Whitelists win where blocklists lose: you cannot accidentally allow something new. When a new binary appears on the system, it is blocked by default. The tradeoff is maintenance — you need to add every tool you legitimately use.
issues.fixed: Whitelists succeed where blocklists fail: you can't accidentally allow something new. New binaries are blocked by default. The tradeoff is maintenance—you need to add every legitimate tool.

issues.original: I have never wanted Claude to push without asking. PreToolUse on [CODE].
issues.fixed: I've never wanted Claude to push without asking. Use PreToolUse on [CODE].

issues.original: PostToolUse on [CODE]. This is hygiene, not policy.
issues.fixed: PostToolUse on [CODE]. This is about hygiene, not policy.

issues.original: The same exit code means very different things depending on the lifecycle phase. Exit 2 only blocks in PreToolUse; in PostToolUse the side-effect already landed.
issues.fixed: The same exit code means different things depending on the lifecycle phase. Exit 2 only blocks in PreToolUse; in PostToolUse, the side-effect has already occurred.

issues.original: PostToolUse runs *after* the edit. Exit code 2 does not roll anything back — the side-effect already happened. Using exit 1 would surface the error to the model, which might then try to 'fix' a formatting issue by re-editing the file, creating a loop. For formatting, just log the warning and move on.
issues.fixed: PostToolUse runs after the edit. Exit code 2 doesn't roll anything back—the side-effect has already happened. Using exit 1 would surface the error to the model, which might then try to 'fix' a formatting issue by re-editing the file, creating a loop. For formatting, just log the warning and move on.

issues.original: This is the single hook that taught Claude to write better code over time on my repos.
issues.fixed: This hook taught Claude to write better code over time on my repos.

issues.original: Exit code 1 in PostToolUse surfaces the failure to the model. The model sees the test output and tries to fix the code. This creates a feedback loop:
issues.fixed: Exit code 1 in PostToolUse surfaces the failure to the model. The model sees the test output and tries to fix the code, creating a feedback loop:

issues.original: PreToolUse on [CODE]. Cheap insurance.
issues.fixed: Use PreToolUse on [CODE]. It's cheap insurance.

issues.original: When you need to recover: I have recovered files from [CODE] exactly twice, both times worth a year of cron job pay.
issues.fixed: When you need to recover, I've used [CODE] exactly twice, both times saving a year's worth of cron job pay.

issues.original: PostToolUse on [CODE] (all tools).
issues.fixed: PostToolUse on [CODE] (all tools).

issues.original: You will not look at this file every day. The day you do, you will be glad it exists.
issues.fixed: You won't look at this file every day, but when you do, you'll be glad it exists.

issues.original: PreToolUse on [CODE]. Forces the model to read a file before editing it.
issues.fixed: Use PreToolUse on [CODE]. It forces the model to read a file before editing it.

issues.original: The hook keeps a small [CODE] map of when each file was last Read. Edits to UNSEEN or STALE files are blocked with a stderr message that tells Claude exactly how to recover.
issues.fixed: The hook maintains a small [CODE] map of when each file was last read. Edits to unseen or stale files are blocked with a stderr message that tells Claude exactly how to recover.

issues.original: This hook needs to be wired on both Read (to track) and Edit/MultiEdit (to enforce):
issues.fixed: This hook needs to be wired for both Read (to track) and Edit/MultiEdit (to enforce):

issues.original: This hook catches the subtle bug where the model edits based on its training data prior, not the file's current state. Without this hook, Claude might 'fix' a function it remembers from training, not the function that actually exists in your repo right now.
issues.fixed: This hook catches the subtle bug where the model edits based on its training data, not the file's current state. Without this hook, Claude might 'fix' a function it remembers from training, not the one that actually exists in your repo.

issues.original: PreToolUse on [CODE].
issues.fixed: Use PreToolUse on [CODE].

issues.original: I run this on the box that handles after-hours pings. If a bot tries to do something destructive at 2am, that is almost certainly a misfire. The hook is not about enforcing work-life balance for Claude — it is about catching runaway automation that should not be running at all.
issues.fixed: I run this on the box that handles after-hours pings. If a bot tries to do something destructive at 2 AM, it's almost certainly a misfire. The hook isn't about enforcing work-life balance for Claude—it's about catching runaway automation that shouldn't be running at all.

issues.original: After hooks, the next thing that changes how Claude Code feels is *concurrency control*. Not concurrency in the threading sense — in the "how many things is the model doing for me, in how much isolation, with how much oversight" sense.
issues.fixed: After hooks, the next thing that changes how Claude Code feels is *concurrency control*. Not in the threading sense, but in terms of how many tasks the model handles, their isolation, and the level of oversight.

issues.original: Plan mode is the cheapest of the three. Press [CODE] until the indicator says **plan**. The model now plans without taking any actions. It will read, think, propose, and stop. You read the plan. You either approve, edit, or kill it. Only then does it execute.
issues.fixed: Plan mode is the cheapest of the three. Press [CODE] until the indicator says **plan**. The model will read, think, propose a plan, and stop. You can then approve, edit, or reject the plan. Only after your approval does it execute.

issues.original: Let me show exactly what happens. Say I am working on an Express API and I want to add rate limiting:
issues.fixed: Let me show exactly what happens. Say I'm working on an Express API and want to add rate limiting:

issues.original: Skipping plan mode "because the task is small." Small tasks have the highest density of "wait, that is not what I meant." A one-line fix that touches the wrong file is worse than a large feature with a clear plan.
issues.fixed: Skipping plan mode because the task is small. Small tasks often lead to misunderstandings. A one-line fix in the wrong file is worse than a large feature with a clear plan.

issues.original: You can steer the plan with instructions: The model respects the scope constraint. This keeps plans from becoming 20-step novels.
issues.fixed: You can steer the plan with instructions. The model respects the scope constraint, which keeps plans from becoming 20-step novels.

issues.original: A sub-agent is a Claude Code instance the parent agent spawns to handle a scoped task. It gets its own context window, its own tool set, and its own instructions. The parent orchestrates; the sub-agents do the work.
issues.fixed: A sub-agent is a Claude Code instance spawned by the parent agent to handle a scoped task. It has its own context window, tool set, and instructions. The parent orchestrates, while the sub-agents do the work.

issues.original: The sub-agent's context window is its own. The parent's stays clean. This is critical when you are working on a complex task and need research done without polluting your main context with 30 file reads.
issues.fixed: The sub-agent's context window is separate. The parent's remains clean. This is crucial when working on a complex task and need research without cluttering the main context with 30 file reads.

issues.original: Spawning a sub-agent is not instant. For a task that takes 10 seconds, the overhead of spawning is not worth it.
issues.fixed: Spawning a sub-agent is not instant. For a 10-second task, the overhead isn't worth it.
